required.title: [Book] Caring for Your Baby and Young Child: Birth to Age 5
required.author: the American Academy of Pediatrics

required.title: [Activity] Real babysitting or child care experience
[Activity] Real babysitting or child care experience

suggested.title: [Book] What to Expect the First Year
suggested.author: Heidi Murkoff

supplementary.title: [Video] YouTube: Safe sleep practices for infants

competence_markers.description: The student can safely hold, feed, change, and soothe an infant
The student can supervise children ages 1-5 with appropriate safety awareness (choking hazards, water safety, fall prevention)
The student can engage children in age-appropriate activities for extended periods
The student can provide competent home care for a sick person: monitoring, hydration, nutrition, medication administration, and recognizing when to seek medical help
The student demonstrates awareness of elder care challenges and can assist elderly people with daily tasks
The student can manage their own emotional responses during caregiving (frustration, exhaustion, worry) without those responses degrading the quality of care
The student has completed a babysitting certification or equivalent training
The student can reliably and safely babysit for a full day

cross_connections.description: Emergency response skills for dependents who are injured or acutely ill. First aid competence is essential for any caregiver.
Caregiving is one of the highest-stress sustained activities a person faces. IC3 provides the emotional regulation and resilience tools.
Patience, selflessness, and doing the hard thing when no one is watching — caregiving tests character in ways public life rarely does.
Feeding dependents requires cooking skills. A caregiver who can prepare appropriate meals for a baby, a sick person, or an elderly person combines 2.3.1 and 2.3.5 competencies.

Understanding what a pre-verbal baby needs, what a confused elderly person is trying to communicate, or what a sick person is too proud to ask for — these require the listening and observation skills from 3.3.
Babysitting is often a first source of earned income. Managing that income connects to the financial habits from 2.3.4.

content.why_this_matters:
In Stretching Branches, you learned that people need care — that babies, elderly people, and sick people depend on others. You practiced gentleness, helping, and noticing needs. That was the foundation. Now you need the skills.

A teenager who can reliably care for a young child for a full day — feeding, supervision, engagement, nap time, safety — is not just babysitting. They are demonstrating one of the most important competencies a human being can have: the ability to keep a dependent person safe, comfortable, and cared for. This skill is not gendered, not optional, and not something that can be learned from a book alone. It requires practice with real people in real situations.

Similarly, the ability to care for a sick family member at home — monitoring their condition, managing hydration and nutrition, administering basic medications correctly, and knowing when to call a doctor — is a skill that most people need multiple times in their lives and that most people never formally learn. They figure it out in a crisis, and the quality of care suffers.

This packet teaches you the practical skills of caregiving: infant and child care, home care for sick people, elder care basics, and the emotional dimension of sustained caregiving. When you finish, you will be a person who can be trusted with the care of someone who cannot fully care for themselves — and that trust will be earned through demonstrated competence, not assumed through good intentions.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Infant and child care skills** — safely holding, feeding, changing, soothing, and supervising young children
2. **Child development awareness** — understanding what children can and cannot do at various ages, and what behaviors are normal
3. **Home care for the sick** — monitoring condition, managing nutrition and hydration, administering basic medications, recognizing when to seek medical help
4. **Elder care awareness** — understanding the realities of aging and how to assist with daily tasks, medication, and companionship
5. **Emotional management in caregiving** — handling frustration, exhaustion, and the psychological demands of sustained care
6. **Babysitting competence** — the ability to reliably and safely care for children for extended periods
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Infant and Child Care Basics

**Holding a baby.** Always support the head and neck — a newborn cannot hold their head up. Cradle the baby in the crook of your arm with your hand supporting the bottom. Or hold the baby against your chest with one hand supporting the head and the other under the bottom. Never hold a baby with one hand. Never leave a baby unattended on any elevated surface — they can roll off a bed, changing table, or couch in a fraction of a second.

**Feeding an infant.** If bottle feeding: warm the bottle by placing it in warm water (never microwave — it creates hot spots). Test the temperature on your inner wrist — it should feel warm, not hot. Hold the baby in a semi-upright position. Tilt the bottle so milk fills the nipple (to prevent the baby from swallowing air). Burp the baby during and after feeding by holding them upright against your shoulder and gently patting or rubbing their back.

**Diaper changing.** Gather everything you need before you start — clean diaper, wipes, cream if used, change of clothes if needed. Never leave the baby alone on the changing surface. Remove the soiled diaper, clean the baby thoroughly (front to back for girls to prevent infection), apply cream if needed, and secure the clean diaper. Dispose of the soiled diaper. Wash your hands.

**Soothing a crying baby.** Babies cry because they need something: food, a clean diaper, sleep, comfort, burping, temperature adjustment. Check each one systematically:
1. When did they last eat? Are they hungry?
2. Is the diaper wet or soiled?
3. Are they too hot or too cold? (Feel their chest — not hands or feet — for temperature.)
4. Do they need to burp?
5. Are they overtired? (Overstimulated babies sometimes need to be in a dim, quiet environment to fall asleep.)
6. Do they just need to be held? Sometimes a baby needs physical comfort — rocking, swaying, patting.

If a baby cries for an extended period and nothing works, and you are alone, it is acceptable to place the baby safely in their crib on their back and step away for a few minutes to collect yourself. A frustrated caregiver is a dangerous caregiver. Never shake a baby. Shaking a baby can cause brain damage or death.

**Safe sleep.** Babies sleep on their backs, on a firm, flat surface, with no pillows, blankets, stuffed animals, or bumpers. This reduces the risk of SIDS (Sudden Infant Death Syndrome). The room should be at a comfortable temperature. A sleep sack (wearable blanket) is the safe alternative to loose blankets.

### Part 2: Supervising Young Children (Ages 1-5)

Young children are mobile, curious, and have no sense of danger. Supervising them means more than being in the same room. It means active awareness of what they are doing and what hazards exist.

**Choking hazards.** Children under four are at high risk of choking. Hazardous items include: small toys or toy parts, coins, batteries (especially button batteries — these are medical emergencies if swallowed), grapes and hot dogs (cut lengthwise, then into small pieces), hard candy, popcorn, nuts, and anything smaller than a toilet paper roll. Know the Heimlich maneuver for children (learned in 2.4.4 First Aid).

**Water safety.** Young children can drown in inches of water. Never leave a child unattended near any water — bathtub, kiddie pool, bucket, toilet. Drowning is fast and silent. It does not look like the movies.

**Falls and injuries.** Gates at stairs. Furniture anchored to walls (bookshelves and dressers tip over on climbing children). Sharp corners padded. Medications and cleaning chemicals out of reach and locked if possible. Electrical outlets covered.

**Age-appropriate activities.** Understanding what a child can do at each age prevents both understimulation and unsafe expectations:
- 1-2 years: stacking blocks, simple puzzles, reading picture books, playing with balls, exploring safe environments
- 2-3 years: coloring, playdough, pretend play, simple games, outdoor play in enclosed areas
- 3-5 years: more complex pretend play, simple board games, art projects, physical play (running, jumping, climbing with supervision), early reading activities

**Managing behavior.** Young children test limits. This is normal and necessary — it is how they learn. Your job is to be consistent, calm, and clear:
- Set simple rules and enforce them consistently
- Redirect rather than punish: "You cannot throw blocks. You can throw this ball."
- Acknowledge feelings without accepting bad behavior: "I know you are angry. You still cannot hit."
- Stay calm. A caregiver who yells or loses control escalates the situation

### Part 3: Caring for Sick People at Home

Most illnesses are managed at home, not at a hospital. The ability to provide competent home care is a practical skill that saves money, reduces unnecessary emergency room visits, and provides comfort that a medical facility cannot.

**Monitoring.** Check on a sick person regularly — every two to four hours during the day, less frequently if they are sleeping well. Take their temperature. Note symptoms: fever, cough, congestion, nausea, pain level, energy level. Write it down. A log of symptoms over time is useful for the sick person, for you, and for a doctor if one is needed.

**Hydration.** This is the single most important element of home care for most illnesses. Fever, vomiting, and diarrhea all cause fluid loss. Push fluids: water, broth, electrolyte drinks (Pedialyte for children, sports drinks for adults), herbal tea. Small, frequent sips are better than large amounts at once if the person is nauseated.

**Nutrition.** Appetite decreases with illness. Do not force food, but offer it regularly. Simple, bland foods: toast, crackers, rice, bananas, soup, applesauce. Avoid dairy if there is congestion (it can thicken mucus). Avoid heavy, greasy, or spicy food if there is nausea.

**Medication.** Administer only medications that have been approved by an adult or prescribed by a doctor. Follow dosing instructions exactly — the right medication, the right dose, at the right time. Use the measuring device provided (not a kitchen spoon — they are inaccurate). Keep a medication log: what was given, how much, and when. This prevents accidental double-dosing.

**Comfort.** Adjust the room temperature. Provide extra blankets or remove them based on the person's comfort. Dim lights if they have a headache. Keep the room quiet. Offer entertainment (books, audiobooks, television) if the person is awake and bored. Sometimes just sitting in the room so they are not alone is the most comforting thing you can do.

**When to seek medical attention.** Call a doctor or go to urgent care if:
- Fever above 103 degrees Fahrenheit (39.4 Celsius) in an adult, or above 100.4 degrees Fahrenheit (38 Celsius) in an infant under three months
- Fever that persists more than three days
- Difficulty breathing (not just congestion — actual labored breathing)
- Signs of dehydration: very dark urine, no urine for eight or more hours, dizziness, dry mouth and lips, crying without tears (in children)
- Severe or worsening pain
- Confusion or unusual drowsiness
- Rash that does not blanch (fade) when pressed
- Vomiting or diarrhea that prevents any fluid intake for more than 24 hours
- Any gut feeling that something is seriously wrong — trust this instinct

### Part 4: Elder Care Awareness

Aging is not a disease. It is a normal process that brings specific challenges. Understanding these challenges prepares you to help — now with grandparents and elderly neighbors, and later in life when your own parents age.

**Common realities of aging:**
- **Declining mobility.** Muscles weaken. Balance deteriorates. Falls become a serious risk — a hip fracture in an elderly person can be life-threatening. Help by: removing trip hazards (rugs, cords), ensuring adequate lighting, offering a steady arm when walking, being patient with slow movement.
- **Cognitive changes.** Memory declines with age. Some forgetfulness is normal. Significant cognitive decline — repeating the same question within minutes, getting lost in familiar places, forgetting the names of close family members, personality changes — may indicate dementia and warrants medical evaluation.
- **Medication management.** Many elderly people take multiple medications. Keeping track of what to take, when, and in what combination is genuinely complex. Pill organizers (weekly or daily), medication lists, and regular review with a pharmacist help prevent dangerous errors.
- **Social isolation.** Elderly people who live alone, cannot drive, or have lost friends and spouses to death often experience profound loneliness. This is not just sad — social isolation measurably worsens physical health and accelerates cognitive decline. Regular visits, phone calls, and invitations to participate in family life are genuine health interventions.
- **Dignity.** Aging brings dependency, which can be humiliating for people who were once fully independent. Helping an elderly person should preserve their dignity: ask before helping (they may want to do it themselves), respect their preferences, listen to their input about their own care, and never talk about them as if they are not in the room.

### Part 5: The Emotional Labor of Caregiving

Caregiving is hard. Not just physically — emotionally. Acknowledging this is not weakness. It is honesty.

**Frustration.** A baby who will not stop crying, a toddler who throws a tantrum, a sick person who is irritable, an elderly person who asks the same question repeatedly — these test your patience. You will feel frustrated. The question is not whether you feel it, but what you do with it.

Strategies:
- Recognize frustration early. When you feel your patience running thin, name it: "I am getting frustrated."
- Take a brief break if safety allows. Put the baby in the crib. Step into the next room for sixty seconds. Breathe.
- Lower your expectations temporarily. A frustrated caregiver trying to be perfect makes things worse. Adequate care delivered calmly is better than perfect care delivered with visible anger.
- Ask for help. If another adult is available, say "I need a break." This is responsible, not weak.

**Exhaustion.** Extended caregiving is physically draining. Sleep deprivation from overnight care compounds over days. The solution is not to power through indefinitely. It is to build rest into the system: take shifts with another caregiver, sleep when the dependent sleeps, accept help when it is offered.

**Emotional weight.** Caring for someone who is suffering — a child in pain, a grandparent declining — carries an emotional weight that accumulates. Talk about it with a trusted adult. Do not pretend it does not affect you. Processing the emotional dimension of caregiving is what makes it sustainable.

### Part 6: Babysitting — Putting It Together

Babysitting is the practical integration of everything in this packet: child care skills, safety awareness, age-appropriate engagement, feeding, and emotional management. It is also often a teenager's first source of earned income, which connects to 2.3.4 (Domestic Finance).

**Before the parents leave:**
- Get emergency contact numbers (parents' phones, neighbor, poison control, pediatrician)
- Learn the children's routines: meal times, nap times, bedtimes, allowed activities, restricted activities
- Know any allergies, medical conditions, or medications
- Know where first aid supplies, fire extinguishers, and flashlights are located
- Confirm rules: screen time limits, food restrictions, off-limits areas of the house
- Know the address of the house (in case you need to tell emergency services)

**During care:**
- Active supervision at all times for young children. This means eyes on them or ears on them, depending on age and activity.
- Follow established routines. Children do better with consistency.
- Engage with the children — play, read, build, draw. Do not sit on your phone while children entertain themselves (or each other, unsupervised).
- Feed according to the parents' instructions. Prepare food safely. Monitor for choking during meals.
- Manage bedtime according to routine. A calm wind-down, a story, lights out at the agreed time.

**If something goes wrong:**
- Injury: provide first aid. Call the parents. If serious, call 911 first, then the parents.
- Illness: if a child vomits or develops a fever, make them comfortable and call the parents.
- Behavioral crisis: stay calm. Remove the child from the dangerous situation. Do not use physical punishment. Call the parents if you cannot manage.
- Emergency: fire, intruder, severe weather — get the children out or to safety first. Call 911. Then call the parents.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Infant Care Competence
**Developing well:** The student handles babies confidently and safely. Head support is automatic. Feeding, burping, and diaper changes are performed correctly without step-by-step reminders. The student can soothe a crying baby through systematic troubleshooting (hungry? wet? tired? needs burping? needs holding?).

**Developing slowly:** The student is still nervous or awkward with infants. They forget head support, struggle with diapers, or become anxious when the baby cries. Handling lacks confidence.

**Diagnostic questions:** How much hands-on time has the student had with infants? Confidence comes from repetition. A student who has held a baby twice will be nervous. A student who has held a baby twenty times will be competent. If development is slow, increase practice opportunities.

### 2. Child Supervision and Safety Awareness
**Developing well:** The student maintains active awareness during child supervision. They identify and manage hazards proactively. They engage the children in activities rather than just watching them. They manage behavioral challenges calmly and effectively. You would trust them alone with a child.

**Developing slowly:** The student is passive during supervision — present but not engaged. They miss hazards. They do not intervene appropriately when children's behavior requires it. They are on their phone instead of actively supervising.

**Diagnostic questions:** During the full-day care project, is the student anticipating needs or reacting to crises? Anticipation (having the snack ready before the child gets hungry, redirecting the toddler before they reach the hazard) is the mark of a developing caregiver. Pure reaction (dealing with meltdowns and accidents after they happen) indicates insufficient engagement.

### 3. Home Care for the Sick
**Developing well:** The student can maintain a symptom log, manage hydration and nutrition, administer medications correctly, and articulate the warning signs that require medical attention. They have provided competent care during a real illness.

**Developing slowly:** The student can recite what to do but has not done it. Or they provided care during an illness but forgot the log, gave medication at the wrong time, or did not monitor hydration.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student had a real opportunity to care for a sick person? If illness has not occurred during the packet period, simulate the scenario: "Your mother has a 101-degree fever. Walk me through the first four hours of care." The student should be able to describe a specific, practical sequence.

### 4. Emotional Regulation During Caregiving
**Developing well:** The student can identify when they are becoming frustrated or overwhelmed and takes appropriate action: stepping away briefly, asking for help, lowering their expectations temporarily. They maintain calm with dependents even when they are stressed internally.

**Developing slowly:** The student's frustration is visible to the dependent — raised voice, rough handling, impatience, withdrawal. Or the student claims to never be frustrated (denial rather than management).

**Diagnostic questions:** After a challenging caregiving session, ask: "What was the hardest part? When did you feel most frustrated? What did you do about it?" Honest self-assessment is the marker. A student who can say "I got really frustrated when the baby would not stop crying, so I put her in the crib and took sixty seconds in the hallway" is developing well. A student who says "It was fine" when you observed them getting visibly agitated is not yet honest about the emotional demands.

### 5. Elder Care Engagement
**Developing well:** The student has spent meaningful time with elderly people, has assisted with practical tasks, and demonstrates respect for their dignity and autonomy. They understand the major challenges of aging and can discuss them with nuance.

**Developing slowly:** The student's elder care experience is minimal. Visits were brief, superficial, or uncomfortable. The student avoids the topic or treats elderly people with condescension.

**Diagnostic questions:** Can the student describe a specific thing they learned from an elderly person during their visits? If every visit was purely transactional (delivered groceries, left) rather than relational (sat, listened, helped, learned), the engagement was insufficient.
